Summary: This study investigates the use of support vector machine (SVM) classifiers with pre- and post-contrast T2 FLAIR and T1WI subtraction to differentiate treatment-related effects (TRE) from glioma recurrence. The results suggest that combining T1WI and T2 FLAIR subtraction maps improves diagnostic accuracy compared to using either modality alone, offering a potential tool for treatment management of postoperative glioma patients.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming to create a representative study population. The criteria include pathological confirmation of glioma, initial appearance or enlargement of T1WI enhancement during follow-ups, and sufficient MRI quality. Exclusion criteria are also defined, such as unavailable pathological results or insufficient diagnostic proof.

Item3.reason: The reference standard includes pathological confirmation of recurrence by second surgical resections, which is a high-quality standard. For TRE, clinical diagnosis criteria are defined based on stability or regression without additional treatment, no progression on conventional MRI and MRS/MRP, and a minimum 6-month follow-up, which represents a reasonable clinical practice-based standard.

Item4.reason: The study involves data from two different centers, indicating a multi-center approach.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is based on conventional T1WI and T2 FLAIR sequences, which are widely used and easily accessible imaging methods for glioma diagnosis and clinical follow-up. The use of contrast-enhanced T1WI and T2 FLAIR also reflects established practices in glioma imaging.

Item6.reason: The study reports the imaging protocol with acquisition parameters, including the type of scanners, sequences used (TSE for T1WI, dark fluid for T2 FLAIR), voxel size, FOV, and contrast administration details. This level of detail ensures replicability of the method.

Item7.reason: The study mentions that patients were eligible when T1WI enhancement initially appeared or enlarged during follow-ups, indicating that the imaging was performed when TRE or recurrence was noticeable for the first time. The clinical diagnosis criteria for TRE included a minimum 6-month follow-up coverage. This suggests an appropriate interval between imaging and reference standard acquisition.

Condition1.reason: The study involves segmentation of the region of interest (ROI) on the subtraction maps, as described in the "Image Preprocessing and Segmentation" section and illustrated in Figure 2. This segmentation is performed manually by neuroradiologists.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation is performed manually by neuroradiologists, indicating that it is not a fully automated process.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, mentioning that every 2D subtraction map with a visible enhancement lesion was segmented manually by two neuroradiologists using ITK-SNAP. The segmentations were then scrutinized by a neuroradiology professor. This provides a transparent description of the segmentation process.

Item9.reason: The segmentation is performed manually, not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The final segmentation is reviewed by a neuroradiology professor, so it is not produced by a single reader or automated tool.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomic features from the segmented ROIs, indicating the use of hand-crafted feature extraction.

Item11.reason: The study mentions image preprocessing techniques, including co-registration of T1WI and T2 FLAIR sequences using SPM8 and subtraction map calculation using the SPM8 "ImaCalc" tool function. Images were normalized by centering at the mean intensity and dividing by one standard deviation. This indicates appropriate use of image preprocessing techniques with a transparent description.

Item12.reason: The study used Pyradiomics for feature extraction, which is a standardized software. The URL is provided.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types extracted, including Shape, First Order, Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix, Gray Level Size Zone Matrix, Gray Level Run Length Matrix, Neighboring Gray Tone Difference Matrix, and Gray Level Dependence Matrix. This provides transparent reporting of feature extraction parameters.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomic features extracted from the images.

Condition5.reason: The study uses SVM, which is not an end-to-end deep learning approach.

Item14.reason: The study mentions removal of redundant features using Pearson correlation coefficient. If the Pearson correlation coefficient value of the feature pair was larger than 0.86, one of them was removed.

Item15.reason: The study mentions the use of recursive feature elimination (RFE) to select features before building the model. This indicates the removal of redundant features.

Item16.reason: The study does not provide a clear justification for the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size. There is no statistical means to demonstrate this.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study mentions that the dataset was randomly split into training and testing sets at a 7:3 ratio. This indicates a proper data partitioning process.

Item19.reason: The study mentions avoiding multicenter MRI standardization bias by enrolling patients in the two affiliations who underwent a similar scan set up on similar Siemens scanners. However, there is no explicit analysis or removal of potential confounding factors.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics, such as AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV, for the classification task. ROC curves are also presented.

Item21.reason: The study includes uncertainty measures in the form of 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the AUC values. The method to derive the distribution is bootstrapping.

Item22.reason: The study does not mention any calibration assessment of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study uses both T1WI and T2 FLAIR, and does not compare it to single imaging set. Therefore, it is not possible to assess whether the use of multi-parametric imaging is justified.

Item24.reason: The study does not include a comparison with a non-radiomic approach or provide proof of added clinical value. The comparison is only between different radiomic models.

Item25.reason: The study does not include a comparison with simple or classical statistical models. The comparison is only between different radiomic models.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set that is sampled from the same source as the training and/or validation sets.

Item27.reason: The model is tested with independent data from two institutions.
